It was a bright and early Sunday morning when I bought this game. As soon as I booted it up, the rain started to fall on my window with small 'taps'. I played the character 'Commando' and, unbeknownst to be at the time, had accidentally activated a secret game mode outside of the main three. I think the name was Burrough Tempest? I can't quite remember.  Starting the run, I spawned into a map titled 'The Residence of TAB', whatever that meant. It was a house. Oddly realistic. When I walked into the house, I saw a figure that looked like my distant cousin, Tristan Burroughs. It was odd, I thought, as I had not seen him since we were children! It must've been a silly coincidence, or a bug, perhaps; But alas, it was not. He spoke to me through the screen, as if he knew I was playing this 'Commando' character. The rain had picked up now, a thunderstorm warning was issued in my district. But it was this what truly unnerved me. He said only one line of dialogue, before the game crashed out of nowhere:  "The monkey bars, climb them and find me."  10/10 because it had a particularly hot character named 'Huntress'.

One of the best games I've played this year.    At first, playing this game seemed like another game where you play as a hero, shooting monsters and getting through stages. While it is, it is also entirely different because of a single factor. RNG (Random Number Generator" completely reframes the way I saw this game. The very concept of randomness. It shapes how good or bad your run will be based on partly how lucky you are. It is such a simple yet fun concept. While your run isn't entirely reliant on your luck, it still is such a huge factor and I'm so glad this is a core mechanic of the game. It makes the game look less tedious and more "random".    As you spawn into your game. You don't know what items you're gonna get, what bosses you're gonna fight, and how lucky you're going to get. For me, this translates perfectly to life. You're born into the unknown world. You don't know what friends you're going to have, what struggles you're going to face, and how you're going to get along. Only thing that matters is how you're going to handle them. My point is, just like in the game even when you have ♥♥♥♥ items it's not the end of your run. You have duplicators, scrappers, and more importantly, You. You are the final line of defense against all odds. The final say whether you want to scrap the item you have or not. You are meant to face obstacles, go through hardships, fight the biggest challenges. It's what makes you, you.    If you need to take time in a stage to get all the loot, drones, and whatnot. Then take your time. But, don't take too much :)    I hope this reaches the audience who needs this.     Oh and btw, 10/10 game.

Every run in Risk of Rain 2 feels rewarding. Dying late into your run is rough, but never enough to keep you from one more run.  Understanding the world isn't integral to your experience with the game, but there's plenty of interesting, funny and beautiful entries to be found in the Logbook if you're keen.   The DLCs add some of my favourite characters and items which I couldn't imagine the game without. Just keep a close eye on the next one, as there's a mixed track record of quality on release.  There's so much room to adapt and modify the game for their skill level, that most people could find a ruleset perfect for them. It's a game you really could play forever.

Risk of Rain 2 Game Review (Disclaimer - This is just my opinion and may be unpopular, or disliked.)  (General) 1. This game is a Roguelike First-Person Shooter. 2. The story is of you as the player going through maps to fight a guy called Mithrix the King of Nothing.  (Positive) 1. There is a lot of work put into the playable character's and their abilities, maps and enemies making it really worthwhile. 2. The game is challanging in a good way and keeps you pushing to do multiple runs and see what you can achieve each time. 3. The gameplay is really fun and if the runs didn't take so long because they really can reach hours for just one you could play it the whole day. 4. The character abilities and how you can stack them is really fun to figure out to see what you come up with to get through to the end goals. (There is no best way of playing and you can really just be you, which is awesome and rare.) 5. The game's graphics, gameplay and movement are done super well and runs smooth. 6. The in game music is a jam and helps with the game to keep it entertaining. (For me it even gets me to zone out and just play, which is relaxing.)  (Negative) 1. The game style playing again-and-again to do different things during different runs that are difficult and lengthy may not be for everyone, but it is really not tedious. 2. The game is difficult and may not be a game worth playing if you are looking for something casual to do with friends or if you play with try-hards. 3. It can take awhile to memorise the items and figure out good combos yourself, which may lead to people looking at other people's combos and that can take away from you finding what is truly best for your play style. 4. The game can get frustrating when you have to do multiple runs to reach a certain goal as one run can take hours, but it is not always the case.

This game is one of the first that comes to mind when talking about a challenging and good roguelike/lite, and that's for a reason. For 25 bucks, you get a ton of playtime and things to do. The game has technically no meta upgrades but does have meta progression.  Nearly every non-character achievement is an unlock for a new item, and even most of the character achievements give you new specials, primaries, and secondaries. It's really great how much they implemented the achievements into something actually worth doing gameplay-wise.  The game is fast, challenging, has a ton of variation through all the different characters and items, nearly endless build possibilities, and all in all is just a great, polished game.  The game is still being worked on and still getting DLC content. If you're even a little bit interested in roguelikes/lites, you should definitely buy this game.

Spawn in. Get good stuff. Do good damage. Enemies scale to a point where even simple rank and file enemies become burdensome and tedious to fight. Get one shotted. Then have an identical run where you win effortlessly. Next game: get ♥♥♥♥ stuff, no hope in winning; big waste of time. If winning/losing is dependent on RNG, why play? Play a roguelike where RNG is a factor but not the key to success.

I'd like to like this game and open to changing my opinion, but this has to be one of the most boring games i've ever played.   The maps are vast, but your character is really slow (and the running mechanic is clunky to the point i wish it wasn't in the game), and also there's almost nothing to look forward to on the map, spare some scarcely dispersed loot crates and statues.  Visually, maps have interesting design, but their surfaces are mostly flat and the aerial perspective is really strong. In contrast with that, enemy corpses mostly don't disappear, and in the absence of almost anything on the map, they can be very distracting because from a distance they can be indistinguishable from loot crates; as for loot crates themselves, they barely change their look after being opened, and if you have a poor memory there's a good chance you'll keep trying to open a crate you already opened.  Furthermore, there's no minimap to orient yourself, and the entrance to the next level can be annoying to find. With all that, I feel like the game could really benefit for smaller but more densely packed maps. Zoomer mentality? Perhaps, but you can't convince me that running across a barren land at 5 mph is good gameplay.   There's very little decision making. When obtaining upgrades, the overwhelming majority of them are given to you without a choice. Rarely you will have a choice to pick 1 of 3 upgrades from a loot container, but this choice is presented to you only via 3D model of the item, without any beforehand text description, so unless you already memorized most items in the game, this is not a choice at all.   The gameplay itself feels like a pretty basic button mashing with little moments of tension across minutes of running across barren lands. I almost wish there was no auto-aim so that there'd be SOMETHING engaging. It features multiplayer, but there's basically very few ways to interact between the players, in fact the optimal strategy seems to be to stay away from each other as far as possible for the majority of the time (at which point you may just be playing solo).  There's very little sense of progress. You get more money, but things cost more, you get more damage, but enemies have more health. I wish high-tier levels featured some (unbuffed) low-tier mobs that you can melt in a second, just to serve as a psychological/cognitive anchor for your progress. Or a way to get many low-tier items for cheap (with the same idea). So far I got to level 4 or 5 and then got one-shot by whatever random mob/boss. Skill issue? Absolutely yes. Is the game interesting enough to keep improving and resolve said skill issue? Absolutely not.  I played very little so maybe there's a mistake or two in the details, but either way, general experience was not good. If there's any mods that improve the experience, please let me know. However, in its vanilla/default state, i cannot recommend it.

Look.  I see a bunch of positive reviews.  So, obviously, people like this game.  Not me, though.  Honestly, I fail to see how anyone does.  The gameplay is interesting enough, but rather bland.  You play one of a handful of characters, each with their ups and downs.  You are sent to do a mission.  Within that mission, you get your upgrades.  That's as far as I got.  There is no explanation of mechanics.  There is no tutorial.  There's barely even an explanation of the "story".  What upgrades you get are for your single run and, as far as I saw, there aren't even "permanent upgrades" or "incremental improvements" to feel like you are achieving something while you are struggling.  Now, I'll concede, perhaps there is a bunch of stuff later in the game I didn't reach.  You can see I didn't run it too terribly long.  My retort to that would be, "Well, then, the game did a poor job giving me a taste of what was to come."  Not even a hint.  I ran a few runs, found the combat to be pretty bland, the community to be fairly unhelpful, and the overall experience to be uninspired.  Perhaps, then, it's "a whole different experience with friends".  My retort to THAT would be, "Well, then, don't advertise this as a single player game.  Because on your own....it SUCKS."  I'm clearly in a minority here; but, this game didn't just "NOT do it for me"...it actively repulsed me.  I fail to grasp why this game is so "loved".  It must have one HELL of a "mid-late game".  I'll never know, because its "early game" is duller than dishwater and there is nothing presented as a "carrot" to lure me.  It's just been stick after stick after stick.  I'm not going to run the risk of going on one mission too many and getting stuck with this in the effort to find what's so appealing about this game.  I'm out.  You failed to grab me.  Gimmie my refund and I'll be on my way.

Very disappointing game, I tried to like it but I just cant.   1. No tutorial - you start the game and you have no clue what to do and guess what neither does the game (it took me 25 minutes to find first teleporter and then even when I knew Iam supposed to look for them I couldnt find them consistently like why does it have to be complicated and theres an option to make portals more visible but why is that not on by default ???), nothing is explained, you must watch 3 hours of youtube content (that may have outdated information) just to learn most basic mechanics and goal of the game. Not to mention the 2 starting character you get when first starting the game are both complete ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ most new player unfriendly character there are. 2. Homelessness simulator - no matter how well you play you are at mercy of RNG and it wouldnt be that terrible if it wasnt the only thing that can win you the game, if you dont get perfect items by stage 3 you might aswell reset because its literally unwinnable. But ok lets say you get the perfect highroll so you will finally win now right ? Wrong some random enemy will spawn inside of you and oneshot or the final boss will with his insane movement speed (just get movement speed yourself lol - I had 6 movement speed items where am I supposed to get more huh?). 3. Items make no sense, descriptions are not provided, descriptions are wrong, most items are either useless or hurtful to your chances of winning. And ok so just dont pick up bad items right ? Well how do you know which items are bad ? They literally dont tell you what the item is or what it does before picking up so you have to remember which low poly pixelated mess corresponds to which item of which there are a lot of items so good luck remembering all of them. 4. Performance - the game is old and my pc is quite good so you would expect the game to run well but no sudden lag spikes after interacting with most things, or when theres too many enemies (I had 6 fps at one point XD). 5. Overall difficulty is just insane I can barely beat lowest difficulty with strongest characters and op builds. Skill issue unlikely, I played simulacrum where they give you infinite items and it was easy reaching wave 50 (on monsoon which is hardest difficulty but I cant win on easiest in normal game ???) which is supposed to be hard or something (good luck having above 20 fps in waves 70+).  Verdict - just buy Megabonk that game is superior in every way.

Overall, it's a great game held back by one HUGE issue.    The crashes.    Unity's FAVORITE thing to do is to randomly crash. I have genuinely not actually finished a run in months because every single time I want to play I open the game, get a really good run going, then Unity decides it's time to ♥♥♥♥ the bed and sends me back to the desktop.    This also rises the issue where   A) If a run crashes none of the data is saved.   B) There is no way to recover a crashed run.  C) There is no way to rejoin a multiplayer crashed run (which is really weird because all the items and data are still saved on the host's end).    The weird thing is that I never have any lag or heavy resource usage, it always just crashes when nothing is happening.    If they can fix this, I recommend this game ten times over, but until that day it's just a frustrating experience I can't recommend.

I wish I didn't feel the way I do.  Most of the time, I love this game.  But every now and then, I find it extremely frustrating and, more importantly, just not fun at all.  And those unfun times are so unsatisfying, they eclipse the fun of the rest of the game.    The premise of the game is simple.  Explore alien worlds, gather supplies and equipment to increase your power level, get as far as you can, then repeat.  The premise is great.  Unfortunately, the actual gameplay is:    Find the teleporter.    And, occasionally, the "find the teleporter" experience is so frustrating and dejecting, it just ruins the entire rest of the game for me.  I can't get excited anymore about a great start to a run because there's that chance that sooner or later one of the teleporters is going to be difficult or impossible to find.  It'll be down some tunnel with a mostly hidden entrance, or under the water, or on one specific island out of a few dozen similar looking chunks of environment.  Maybe sometimes it's hidden in a place that's impossible to find.  I don't know for sure if that's ever the case, but I do know that I've spent 40 minutes looking for the thing without finding it.    And that's the heart of it.  A decent run of RoR2 takes around 45 minutes give or take.  That's not a huge commitment, but it's a nontrivial investment of time.  And when I only have one break in my day that I can spend gaming, and I spend that time on RoR2, and I end up in a worse mood than when I started... then this just isn't a game I can recommend.

Decent bones, but too many frustrating points:  > Too much downtime between enemy waves and frustration when map rng hides the objective very well, really slows pacing of the game that encourages one to clear a level quickly  > Inconsistent enemy damage that can lead to unexplained deaths during a good run, specific enemies deal a disproportionate amount of damage and can spawn in behind (audio isn't very weighty and hard to discern dangers)... wish the fatal damages were better telegraphed.  > Not a lot of opportunities for choice, it's hard to differentiate the loadout and you're mostly left to the whims of the run.  > Itemization doesn't really differentiate successful gameplay, you can loot and get a run that has more mobility and do well, but tanking... not so great. Damage items hardly change how you approach felling enemies, you just select a diversity to get the best damage stacks and hope the RNG is in your favor to make it quicker rather than slower.  > Information is extremely vague, you won't know what a new to you item will do until you pick it up, which can be a pain as some have drawbacks that you can't easily rid of.   > Some playtesting seems to be lacking, some side levels require a lot of waiting before you can get the item to progress, the downtime before enemy waves that you need to kill to progress is not excusable, it just bogs down these specific side-levels.    That said some positives:  > The general character design does have good variation between the characters, where some characters have a more rewarding gameplay loop other than shoot sprint next area shoot.   > The level's art direction and music is pretty well done (not a graphical masterpiece, but the setting, props, music etc fit well together).

You die, and die, and die again.   Dying is one gameplay to unlock progressively better items to help players beat a game that is harder than an average game to the point you dont die anymore. Binding of Isaac, Into the Gungeon, Peglin..,  Then you have difficult roguelikes that you need to git gud, and you unlock goodies the more gudder you git. This type of genre doesn't fall into either category, but instead falls into the "you must kill everything or you die" category. Obtain every item to kill the entire screen or you die. But for a game like this, and vampire survivors, and super bonk.., there is no way to describe the gameplay or the exact description on how you'd describe this game, better than that one single phrase. Risk of Rain 2 is wildly overrated.  Risk of Rain 2 is one of those games people swear gets amazing once you “learn it,” but after playing it, I mostly came away thinking it hides bad design behind chaos and randomness.  The UI is terrible, item descriptions are awkward to inspect, and the game does a bad job of giving you information in a way that feels smooth or intuitive. For a game this dependent on item knowledge, that is a huge failure.  The gameplay does not feel skillful so much as random and messy. A good run feels more like luck than mastery, and a bad run often just feels like the game drowning you in unreadable chaos. That is not satisfying.  The worst part is the item and map design. Running all over the map for loot is boring, and in multiplayer it actively makes the game worse because you end up competing with your friends for resources. That is such a stupid design choice for co-op.   Then late game turns into complete garbage. The screen becomes a disaster of effects and enemies until the whole thing stops being fun and starts being noise.  Cool idea, bad execution.

After giving Risk of Rain a chance multiple times, with long breaks in between, and ending up disappointed again and again, I think I'm ready to accept that it's not me not getting the game — it's the game being poorly designed in so many ways that I find it crazy how many people praise it so highly.  Initially, I bought it to play in co-op with my little brother. Very quickly it became obvious that it was a terrible idea. For some reason, you cannot revive teammates. If one of you dies, the other has to wait until the end of the stage, which can take 10-30 minutes to complete. That alone is bad enough, but it doesn't end there. While your teammate is dead, you keep running around, collecting power-ups and leveling up. The progression isn't shared, so if there is a skill gap, it widens even further. Now guess what's going to happen once you reach the next stage. If the person who died already struggled with the previous level, how are they going to perform after getting thrown against harder enemies with no upgrades at all? Well, if you didn't guess, they’ll probably die again. Very quickly. So co-op Risk of Rain ends up being a spectator simulator for the weaker player in your group, and there is almost nothing you can do to improve their experience. Not fun.  What about the gameplay loop itself? The game has one positive aspect in my eyes — it offers a decent roster of heroes with unique playstyles. But... they all have something in common. No matter who you pick, a large portion of your playtime is spent just running around uninspiring maps, trying to find crates with upgrades. Personally, I find this extremely boring and tedious. And don't get me started on finding a portal. I once spent 30 minutes aimlessly wandering all over the cleared level, only to find it in the most ridiculous place imaginable. Why not add a marker after X minutes on a level? The game is devoid of basic quality-of-life features you'd expect as a new player. I come to the shop with three items I can choose from — why on earth is there no description explaining what each one does? I find a recycler to get rid of unwanted power-ups. I open it and start checking item descriptions to make a decision, but the game doesn't pause, so I can get killed while looking through my items. Why?   Nothing ever gets explained in-game, so if you, like me, prefer to experience games organically without guides or wikis, Risk of Rain can be very frustrating. For example, about 30 minutes into one of my runs, I teleported into some kind of dream dimension for the first time. I wandered around a bit, bought a couple of items, and went through the first portal I found. It teleported me into an area where I took constant damage until I died in about 10 seconds. The run was over. No explanation of what happened or why. Like what the hell is that?   And if your run doesn't end like this, most likely you will get one-shotted by some random ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. Imagine playing a run for about an hour: you're pretty happy with how strong you’ve become, maybe even having some fun. I will preface this review by saying that I don't think this is a bad game. However, I made the mistake of accepting this game as a gift from a friend without knowing anything about it. I played it with a couple friends for about 2 hours, and in that time, I felt like I did not understand anything that was going on, nor did I feel like I was ever given time to just figure things out because the game refuses to allow the player any time to catch their breath, let alone get a feel for things at their own pace.  I found the game was poor at communicating how most things worked, but I also understand that that's probably by design since it's a run-based game and you're meant to figure out what abilities and boosts work best for you by just using them a lot and seeing what happens. These kinds of games are not for me, and I will not be playing it ever again.  I would like to think that most people who buy this game know what they're getting into, but on the off chance someone reading this doesn't know that this is a relentlessly fast-paced game where you need to constantly be doing something to stay alive, do not buy this game if that doesn't sound fun or interesting to you.  EDIT: With the comments I have received, I suppose I should clarify that I did initially try to play the game solo for about 10-15 minutes, and my friends could tell I was hopelessly lost and did not know what I was doing or even what my basic objective was, which was when we started a multiplayer session. I would like to reiterate that I do not think this is a bad game; I just think it's extremely unintuitive and frustrating for players new to this type of game, which is why I would not recommend it to most people. I'm sure it's great if you're willing to put in the time to learn how everything works, but I actively hated my time with the game too much to try to get to that point. I do not find the core gameplay loop to be at all enjoyable or compelling. It is not my thing, and that's totally okay.

Risk of Rain 2 has been my favorite game that I've picked up over the past three or four years (with the exception of Elden Ring). I love genuinely love the game. To me it's the best rogue-like I've played and that primarily stems from my multiplayer experiences with friends. However, because of recent bugs introduced into the game, I have to leave a negative review. With the release of Alloyed Collective a lot of existing bugs were fixed and a ton of of content was added. However, the unification of the console and PC code base seems to have created an issue where multiplayer no longer works for a portion of people (myself included). The host of a multiplayer game can experience the game perfectly but anyone who plays with them experience over ten seconds of lag at any given moment. The game is stuttery and completely unplayable. You can watch the host play just fine (minor stuttering) but you can't do anything. I love this game, but I can't recommend it because it's greatest feature is now broken and has been for months. Upvote this comment if you're experiencing similar issues to boost awareness. Thx. All the best. -Tubby

**edit** changing to a negative review due to issues I am starting to see as I play more and understand the game better. The lying in tooltips for certain characters (bandit going invisible; which does not make you invisible to enemies lol?) is an instant negative review for me as I do not know of a single reason to mislead your players. That and learning that the devs intentionally kept in items spawning off the side of a cliff (making them inaccessible) because they thought it is funny. Its too bad as the underlying game is good, but asinine dev choices seem to intentionally want to sink the fun in their game!  Fun game with a repetitive but addicting loop. Even more fun with friends but is absolutely solo friendly. I like the achievement mechanic to unlock new characters, as its fun and I have yet to be asked to open my wallet for a character. Great art-style and some good music to go with it, especially the 'end' boss area, how it goes from slow and somber to slowly ramping up  the beat.  My biggest issue with it is that you can not save in between rounds or really at any point. Need to go out to get some things done? Better leave that computer on and wasting power. I have heard people try to rationalize why its not in there but I find it unconvincing. It just seems like a big oversight. If I already proved I can beat the level, and can pause for that matter, then why not let me have a temp save point that deletes on death?  My second issue is that there should be an option to add in tooltips. I find it extremely unintuitive for the first few runs trying to understand what each of the items do. All you get is their name so I end up ruining a run trying out a new item or just pausing to head to the wiki. I never make excuses for games that require a 3rd party source to understand in game functions.  Edit: I feel I need to add in that the bandits invisible move does not actually make you invisible. So I guess watch out for the descriptions you read as they may be misleading. I was playing with a few others on monsoon and was the last survivor. I could almost always stay invisible due to items, yet enemies knew where I was and where I would be, at all times. It is kind of a ball buster when your move says you go invisible and then enemies track you better than Predator's shoulder cannon all the same.   *** I also wanted to add I have tested it further by zigzagging during a laser attack from the stone golem boss ( I stacked movement to be as effective as possible). Its laser followed me, without breaking off me, throughout the entire shift ability use. This is really a bummer to find out how bad the ability is and leads me to ask, did they nerf this ability into the dust? Honestly the more I play, the more I lean towards wishing there was a neutral review. Still a good game but with some decisions by the devs that left me scratching my head.

I really tried to understand the hype around Risk of Rain 2 – multiple attempts, different characters, all the patience in the world. But at some point you've got to admit it: this game and I, we're not gonna be friends.  70% Running Simulator, 30% Gameplay  The maps are massive. And empty. You run, look for chests, run some more, hope you didn't miss anything, run to the teleporter. The fights in between? Shoot, dodge, repeat. No weight behind the weapons, no impact – this feels like a souped-up Flash game, just with better visuals.  A roguelike should hook me from minute one, not after 40 minutes when you've finally scraped together enough items. The early phase of every run is pure chore work: creeping across the map, checking every corner, hoping the timer doesn't eat you alive. That's not gameplay – that's work.  Huge arenas, generic enemies, zero atmosphere. No sense of discovery, no "oh, what's this?". Just: find the items before the difficulty spike kills you. Maybe optimization fans are into that – for me it feels like a job I don't want.  At least I'm glad I didn't pay full price on Steam. On sale it was bearable, but even with the discount, those hours remain wasted time.

Okay... I know I don't have that much hours in this but I pretty much get the gist of it. You go into a random-generated world, try to find the teleporter, and while doing so you take out some enemies to earn money, and you use that money to buy some gear that will make you become stronger. And once you activate the teleporter, swarms of enemies spawn in as well as a big gigantic boss that you have to take out. Oh, you also have to be near the teleporter in order for it to charge up. And once it's fully charged, you take it, all your money goes away for some reason, and then you teleport to a new world and it all just repeats. How is that freaking fun?  I mean don't get me wrong, it's cool for the first 10 minutes, but once that honeymoon phase is over, it gets boring after a while. Fighting the same enemies in the same worlds with the same loot like... same, same, same 24/7. Barely any variety to keep it fresh with no incentive to play different characters. Especially considering that Bandit is the best character to play early on. Like everyone else is obsolete compared to this guy. I guess it's because he's a new character but still. Couldn't they buff the other characters?  Now, for my next issue, why are the worlds so dang big? I mean I wouldn't really have a problem with that if it wasn't for the fact that we don't get a map to see if we've been in an area before or not, or how we move extremely slow (unless you get lucky with the increased sprint speed cans), or how the biggest one of them all... is how the teleporter doesn't reveal itself if we haven't find it in a very long time. Like how the heck does that work?  You don't just make these big randomly-generated worlds, make the teleporter spawn randomly, and then expect every single player to not spend 10 minutes trying to find this thing. Like I was literally in this one world called Distant Roost trying to find this teleporter and after God knows how long, I decided to just randomly look down into the abyss, and apparently the teleporter was down below where I was looking. Like who the heck puts land 20 feet beneath some land in a world where the abyss is always present?  Also, back to what I mentioned before, I really don't like how you move so slow in this. Why do we move so slow? Now, you may say, "oh just spam the movement utility", but if the devs knew we were gonna do that for world navigation, why didn't they give us something designed for that? 'Cause having to go through an infinite loop of... "use movement utility, stops, goes on cooldown. Use movement utility, stops, goes on cooldown" is exTREMELY annoying. Like seriously, this game is set in some future nonsense, the characters got a huge ship in space, and you mean to tell me they can't bring a jetpack with infinite fuel? Like come on.  To make matters worse, because of all of these factors you got, your runs will last for a very long time. Like my longest run was like 40 minutes long when I was in stage 3 and I need to get to stage 5 in order to get to the final boss and beat the game. Meaning your runs will take at least an hour unless you decide not to navigate the world and ignore all the upgrades for some reason. And I say, "for some reason" 'cause not only do you need these upgrades to have a chance against this final boss (or any boss for that matter), but that's really the main gimmick of this game. It's literally in the store description. "Combine loot in surprising ways" and you choosing not to do that like... what did you buy this game for then?  Now, you may be shocked but yes, I have another issue. And it's not the gameplay, it's the music this time. Now I know I know, "What? You don't like the music? It's the biggest praise for this game!" No, no, I do like the music. It's just this one beat drop that I don't like in 1 specific song. It's the one that plays when you're in the Distant Roost world. The Evapotranspiration song. What the heck is that beat drop at 3:31? Like the song sounds well at first but then when it gets to that part, this loud drumming sound comes out of nowhere. Just starts playing in the background like it belongs there. Get the heck out of here man like no one asked you to be here. Now, you may say, "oh it's just a preference", but just like people looking at children on google, not all preferences are right. Seriously, I know this dude was getting praise by the devs for his music, but I think he got a little too ego-driven and thought he was cooking when he put this unnecessary drum beat into this song. Like this literally sounds disgusting.  But yeah man, I really am upset with all of this man like... you got repetitive gameplay, a normal run could last for an hour, constant loop of using your movement utility just to navigate when they literally could've just given you a jetpack with infinite fuel for the occasion, if you spend too long trying to find the teleporter, welp, continue searching man. The devs don't care if it takes you 20 minutes. They're not revealing this teleporter to you 'cause they think it's a skill issue even though they made the teleporter spawn randomly in a random generated map. And on top of that, once you do find it, the devs didn't make it where like... it stays revealed to you on your HUD after you have found it. It only stays revealed to you after you activated it. Now you may say, "well then why not just activate it and then continue looking for upgrades afterward?" Okay for number 1, you're risking the chances of dying to the horde and the boss if you don't have good enough upgrades to overcome them. And for number 2, even if you do overcome them, enemies will no longer spawn for some reason meaning you could come across some upgrades but you can't buy them 'cause you don't have enough money. And you can't get any more money 'cause no more enemies will spawn meaning you're forced to get into the next harder stage without the upgrades you could've acquired.  Like honestly, I don't know how this game got tons of glaze considering the dozens of flaws it has. Like I know all games are meaningless but still, why make the experience within them unbearable? Like there's literally no reason for them to force us to navigate slowly in these randomly-generated worlds while trying to find a randomly-spawned teleporter that doesn't stay revealed once found nor gets revealed if we haven't found it after several minutes. And then they have the audacity to not allow you to choose what to put in a 3D printer even though they allow you to choose what to scrap. How the heck does that make any sense? Like our character has to for some reason throw a random item into the printer like they'll explode or something if they don't. But when it comes to scrapping an item, oh yeah they can choose now for some reason.  Like seriously, I'm sorry for wasting your time reading all of this but I really had to go off after being hype-trained. This game is literally straight up worse than Gunfire Reborn. Like yeah, don't get me wrong, Gunfire Reborn has that repetitiveness as well, but considering it has better variety, more balanced characters, and better builds to come up with... it surprises me how it's cheaper than this game. Especially considering that it looks better like seriously. Say what you want about these graphics, but in my humble opinion, these 3D graphics are freaking disgusting. Not as bad as FlyKnight though, but still disgusting. Like I had that same opinion years ago and after now buying it and getting into it... I still think these graphics are disgusting. How the heck did they make it look like this? Don't get me wrong, I don't expect them to be God or anything and make these graphics be so high in detail that it looks like real life but still. How the heck they look at this and said, "Oh yeah. This will do"? Like what the-  But yeah, I'm roasting this game again, sorry for wasting your time. Just do yourself a favor and don't buy this game. Get Gunfire Reborn instead.

Here is why:  1: Repetitive gun sounds on one of the two unlocked characters that came with the game. the other character called "Huntress" (i think lol) has a somewhat satisfying SFX  2: Enemies respawning non stop where you just killed them. Ok here is some things i like about it:  1: interesting models for enemies that looked quite unique  2: the affects of upgrades were pretty good and the amount of upgrades you get per "level" was good  3: Special abilities on characters help mix up the strategy in the fighting. I bought this game with high expectations, but i did not end up enjoying it. I experienced the game once more but i still could not get to like it entirely.  The gameplay became repetitive quite quickly for me. The concept of having character with abilities cooldowns is cool, but for the type of game the kits feel limited in depth, this is also problematic if you think that the gameplay rely heavily on itemizations rather than growing along the abilities you have, which is, another thing i do not like. The main objective is to survive then get to the portal, it would be cool if there were more objectives, because the enemy variety started to feel repetitive, add that to the fact that you have to find the teleporter for some time which can be tedious. Boss fights were pretty fun, though, and the soundtrack is rock solid. It would be cool if you could join matches in progress to an extent since the lobbies are almost empty.  Overall, i just can not recommend the game, this is just based on my experience. After several years of updates, i expected more depth of the core game and specific kind of content that i previously talked about, that is important for making a fun game. This game has a lot of potential, a lot, but it did not work for me given the current state.

i cannot recommend this game at all, to be quite honest. i have so many issues in so many aspects of the game. not big enough issues to make me stop playing, but big enough to where i will complain about every single aspect of the game relentlessly. i will spare this review from that fate, however, and simply list off some of the more egregious issues (and how i would address them):  -every character is far too weak and slow at the start of runs, making the game feel sluggish (multiply the base speed by 1.5x)  -melee characters are completely nonfunctional due to the prevalence of flying enemies (give all melee characters a weak projectile tied to their primary attack)  -auto item pickup is irritating (add an option to turn it off)  -elites are far too tanky, and far too common (make them far rarer, and reduce their health to be more in line with regular enemies)  -every final boss takes far too long to defeat (cut each of them down to 1-2 phases)  -the gradual difficulty scaling fails at encouraging playing fast, and only punishes exploration (get rid of it, leaving only the per-level difficulty)    if you find this game on sale, and really want it that bad, then go ahead. but i would never pay full price for a game like this, not with the massive pile of issues it comes with.

Honestly I do like this game I'm giving it a negative review not because it's a bad game but because it fails at being a good sequel. Risk of rain returns is a much better choice. The game to me just doesn't translate well to 3d. For one thing enemies spawning around you is more noticeable since.. they will literally follow you to the ends of the earth. I found this annoying compared to the other game because with the first game it still gave you a chance to explore. You had time to look around for chests and other things despite the large amount of combat involved. Whereas in 2 you can't pause for 5 seconds. This might be partially due to me playing specifically singleplayer since I believe the game was designed with multiplayer in mind. And that's the other thing exploration can be really frustrating in risk of rain 2 since generally the levels are a solid.. 8x the size of one of the 2d worlds and thus take much longer to explore. Despite this difficulty goes up at the exact same rate so you're pretty much doomed to fail. This sequel also removed the pixel art charm that the first game had. It's not even a bad game by any means I just don't think it's a faithful sequel, it doesn't evoke the same kind of experience even remotely. Risk of rain returns however is a perfect sequel. It is extremely similar to risk of rains original side scroller format but with more enemies characters and items added, along with updated graphics and large gameplay improvements.

Compared to other roguelikes ive played this one is just mid by comparison. On its own its a decent and addicting game but just bland in my opinion.     First of all, how is there no option to save and finish the run later?! They could have easily let you save at the start of each level but no. Not everyone has the time to complete a run all in one go. Secondly, the item variety is just meh here with more emphasis on stacking items which means that by the end of your 3rd run you have probably seen all the basic items and a bunch of green ones too but to be fair the legendary items are still rare and exciting which adds replayability. However, the fact that you can easily go from full HP to dead in a matter of seconds just makes me wonder if I lost due to not being able to dodge or just that I didn't get lucky and stack enough heals. The difficulty system here is unique and normally I am against time pressure, here it works because you can leave at any time but can you find all the chests and loot before the difficulty gets unbearable? Thats something you will have to play around with which adds a layer of strategy to the gameplay. Next, the music can make or break games for me and the music here is not really anything memorable too its just calm and mellow which is strange because runs in this game are chaotic and overstimulating and the music just doesn't reflect this. The characters are great with each one having a unique playstyle and abilities. I just wish you could upgrade or change around abilities during runs because spamming the same 4 abilities the entire run is not very interesting but at least you can find some great ultimate abilities as a 5th slot to keep things fresh.    Overall, I can tell this game was made to be replayable and addicting but it gets stale real fast.

[h1] Negative Reviews for Games You Like: The Obligatory Disclaimer [/h1]  obligatory disclaimer was read, now you read    This game is not optimized for melee characters. Thankfully, there's only one. Only one melee character of a now large roster of almost two dozen characters.     No. Loader is not a melee character. There's a plot twist for you.    [h3] But wait, there's easy! Erm, Drizzle [/h3]  Okay, sure. But that applies to every character. Every character has a learning curve, some start worse and some start immediately powerful.     [h3] Wait. When you mean one melee character...You don't mean... [/h3]  Mercenary. This character was in the original, which was 2D. This is 3D.    In 3D, melee combat is way different and you need to use the geometry to your advantage. Games like Ninja Gaiden come to mind, which as you can see on Steam reviews has found ways to make the combat easier but more engaging. This also applies to Devil May Cry, and yes those are very different games. But Mercenary takes after them. Figure that one out.    Risk of Rain 2 ignored how its inspirational blueprints changed and didn't see the warning signs for keeping Mercenary too faithful. You can pick all of the ranged skills (after unlocking them) and get lucky with the items and pickups. But you want to know what seals the deal completely? Picking artifacts, which make things easier or more difficult.    If you do the artifact run to let you pick items, have more enemies spawn, and items drop from enemies, you're basically playing Debug Mode. Drizzle with Debug Mode is more manageable but feels off. But medium and hard? It's not only pain, it's sobering pain. This is even more noticeable when you make all enemies elite, which really changes your perspective on things. RoR2 prides itself on difficulty, and the reality is that an Elite run even with Debug Mode on, the mercenary can't do anything.     The Mercenary has to get in the faces of enemies, which are designed to punish you with when in proximity. Elite enemies have shorter wind-up times for attacks. You can't dodge effectively when enemies drop damage zones. You are too maintenance-oriented when you can't dodge, block, counter, or deal out decent damage to warrant the punishment. RoR2 is designed to punish you fast and immediately if enemies catch up. This stays consistent even when on a God Run. Don't Get Caught. Oh wait, Mercenary always gets caught.    So...while the Mercenary gets a free pass as the weird challenge character, most people defend this challenge character when it's just a euphemism for broken in the worst way possible. He was cool in part one, but in part 2, you will replace his sword with a moon item that lets you shoot. You do this, because you know he doesn't function as a character. Mercenary is the excuse for picking moon items. What an amazing character. He doesn't function until you replace his skills.    By the way, Loader can launch from one side of the stage to the other after a few items. Mercenary can... "disappear from combat for a second," while still surrounded by enemies...who don't die...who wait patiently for him to come back. What an amazing character.     Mercenary 2.0 when? Never. He's a legacy character and "fixing" him will cause all the wrong people to get mad.

I like a lot of aspects of this game, but I'm having a really hard time getting into it. The most infuriating part about it is the fact, that you have to rely on teleporters to progress. Those can spawn randomly all around the map, sometimes partially buried into the terrain. A relatively small, dark circle barely sticking out from the ground is infuriatingly hard to spot.     Not to mention, the difficulty of the game grows as more time passes. This could be a cool mechanic, letting the player choose if they want to engage in more exploration and get stronger early, or rush the first level or two, to get further without the difficulty growing too much. This choice gets diminished by how the teleporters spawn, which feels like an intentional choice to waste time for anybody playing this game.    I tried getting into this game a few times, but I can't help but feel the strange mixture of boredom and frustration, as I'm running all across an already explored map for up to 25 minutes, trying to find a way to progress. I know that there are sparkles coming off the teleporter to help with locating it, but most of the time, even at a high vantage point, looking over the area, it may not be within the visible area, adding to the frustration and being fed up of running around pointlessly.

Althought the gameplay and combat are solid, the fact that there is no really meta-progression is a con for me...It's pretty much ''git gud'' (and can unlock Artifacts at some points) but ain't time for that unfortunately.

EULA contains anti-consumer policies.  Specifically, requiring you to waive your legal rights (mandatory arbitration for conflict resolution...no right to trial or, if I understand correctly, class action lawsuit).  You may opt out of this clause but only by basically sending certified mail to their offices.  If I can click to accept, I should be able to click to opt out.  These are both increasingly weaponized strategies to screw people over.    They also reserve the rights to change the EULA at any time and, if anything else they include in it is unacceptable to you, you've effectively lost access to a game you've paid for.  This is also common, and something we should start saying no to.   Say no to waiving your legal rights.  Support Click-to-Decline and Click-to-Cancel.   Don't play this game without actually reading the EULA.  The parts I refer to are near the bottom.

This might be the worst roguelight on the market today, and I genuinely regret wasting the time on it I have. Why?    The game is sadistic, plain and simple. It oozes out of every pore of the gameplay, EVEN including the hints. The very first lesson you get: "Take too long, and the enemies will get stronger!" "Don't waste time!" These are lies. You cannot blitz through the game, because RNG will punish you. Have no items? Tanky enemies will spawn and the time it takes you to kill them without damage buffs is so long you would have spent the same time getting BUFFS AND KILLING THEM that you did on just killing them.    The most basic enemy, the wisp, can spawn behind you, out of your line of sight, MID-ATTACK ANIMATION and hit you before their audio warning for spawning EVEN FINISHES. These attacks are borderline impossible to dodge without the use of a block or bunny-hop spam. Because some moron gave hitscan to a basic mob.    Bosses "scale" over time, but the game intentionally wastes as much of yours as possible. The portals take FOREVER to charge up, so rushing the boss down is meaningless. You get no reward for playing well. Only more of your time wasted.    Speaking of RNG: There are two horrific systems in this game that make my eyes roll into the back of my head: Portals and Boss Spawns.    The portals can be added to the teleporter by achieving certain small "goals," but most of them are horrible pains in the butt that waste your time to get! In a game about getting loot as fast as possible to scale up with the enemies, it sure would be so FUNNY and WACKY if the game devs hid randomly spawning newt statues in places unreachable for over half the roster! That would be SO WACKY~ So FUNNY~ It would also be funny if those characters would benefit the MOST from getting it!? But also, haha, what if the newt statue doesn't spawn at ALL and you wastes MINUTES looking for it in SUPER HIDDEN spots because you need one of those items!? HAHAHAHAHAHA~    There are so painfully few teleporter boss enemies that the Devs could be mistaken for a recycling plant. Do you like TITAN or BEETLE MOM? Too bad! That's ALL you will see 99% of the time. Don't worry, though! You can fight multiples of them AT ONCE so you can visually see the re-used content before your very eyes~! Three expansions in and we can't be bothered to add more variety to the BASE gameplay? No. You need more RNG in your items, of course!! Have 20 more items in the pool!    Bosses start to spawn as basic enemies as the time scales up. Sure would suck if the bosses that do spawn are boring slop that spam laser attacks or mobs. With the ability to spawn as elites. With HP so high they actually become tankier than their non-elite, normal boss counterparts. Haha. And like, imagine if they could spawn as HEALING elites!? That's so funny! Haha! And like, imagine if bosses weren't coded to be unhealable by these elites!? TEEHEE!? Image if the wave of elites that spawn in around the boss were all copies of the boss that are ALL HEALING ELITES!? That would be SO WACKY~?!    Every basic game design rule was ignored for this game, and it makes me so sad. There is so much potential in this game to be fun, but I am convinced the game devs were too busy thinking of stupid ways to kill the player. I so painfully want to like the game. It has so much charm and love clearly put into it... but the flaws are so upsetting that it makes me want to eat drywall.      For those looking for a fun read on why I'm writing this? I was 35 minutes deep into a Monsoon run. Half-decent build. Definitely no god run, but good enough to finish with. I do the portal before the 4th world and 7 bosses spawn. Why? I don't know. Mountain wasn't active. Immediately, the game spawns 2 entire waves of elite enemies, over half of them healing elites. The bosses are functionally immortal. I spent 20 minutes kiting and slowly killing the healers, beat the bosses, and went into the blue portal. I clearly needed more items, perhaps even Heretic if it was that rough. Despite LOOKING AT THE BLUE PORTAL, with the text prompt for BLUE PORTAL, it sends me into the gold one behind me. I spawn in and there are already 15 enemies on the ground. 12 of them are Halcyonites, with 3 being healing elite Halcyonites. The other 3 are stone titans.     Genuinely. What the absolute hell am I supposed to do against 15 bosses that all spam lasers, 3 of which are literally undodgeable, and heal each other? Sure, the healing Halcyonites could be slowly chipped down if I had the mental stamina left, but I JUST left a 20 minute grindfest. I tried for 5 minutes, realized I had about 3 seconds out of every 60 to attack safely, just gave up, and let my character die.    I don't care if it was "possible" to do. I care that it wasn't fun in ANY way. If I so much as touch this game again, I will NEVER be playing without cheat engine. I shouldn't have to cheat to make the game fun, but I have just as little respect for a game willing to waste an hour of my life as it does for me.    Tl;Dr - Go play Crab Champions. As someone who adored this game and played the ♥♥♥♥ out of it on both console and PC from pre launch to survivors of the void, I cannot in any ways recommend you purchase it as the IP itself now belongs to some of the most scummy corporations in gaming, and that was clearly illustrated in how they destroyed the far more stable PC version of the game, and superimposed their buggy, FPS locked console port retroactively onto it.  But! you may be thinking, didn't they patch it? Surely it's all fixed now right, why the hate?  To which I direct you to take-two's EULA for not just this game, but everything they have a hand in. Even in absentia of the horrific anti-consumer practices that gearbox themselves are guilty of going back as far as them embezzling funding from SEGA to use on their own projects, if you look at the EULA that Take-Two is now making mandatory on virtually all of their games you will find that they have declared for themselves the rights to access and collect the following information:  -Your name -Your Email -Your phone number -Your photo -Your mailing address/zip code -payment or purchase info -age -gender -password -platform ID -Software products played -Survey data -geolocation -and the systems or platforms you play their product on/through.  Furthermore, they also "share" (read:sell), your information with the following:  -Vendors that perform services and support for them -Take-Two group members -Advertisers and third party advertising partners -Other third parties  I realize that I'm going to most assuredly get flamed for daring to mention these things, likely by a bunch of addict lobotomites who are perfectly willing to sell their privacy, and any dignity or self-respect under the auspices that no one will actually come to collect on them personally, but as someone who purchased this game and played it for ages long before the IP was unceremoniously sold off and gutted by a company I despise due to their long history of abusing their customers, I cannot overstate how much I despise this entire series of events.  And yes, the truth is we're all trapped in this awful market environment, run roughshod over by corporate interests and information brokering thieves, but to have it so blatantly done to a game I loved, and considered one of the best in the genre? To see it literally become unplayable in a proper state for a year before they actually fixed things, and have everything unique and fun about it be dragged out and abused for all it's worth by unscrupulous corporate asshairs who couldn't give a ♥♥♥♥ less about something that was until that point, crowd funded and beloved by its community? It's like purchasing a car and then a decade later the company changes hands and they break into your house and reengineer every part of your vehicle to function worse so they can sell you back the parts they just stole from you. We aren't renters, we are owners of these things, at least we damn well should be for the amount of money we pay for these products. Enough is enough.  I cannot, and will not support this, and I say if you have any respect for yourself or what this game was before they infested it, cut them off. Let something new take its place someday, and God willing let that indie dev not decide to sell out their baby to the devil.

Risk of Rain 2 doesn’t feel satisfying to play: it becomes repetitive, build development is uninteresting, and progression feels slow. There’s no real sense of danger or urgency.    Some thoughts: the teleport event seems pointless—its only function is to move you to another biome, but there’s no meaningful reset or shift in pacing. Boss fights are significantly harder than regular encounters, yet the reward is the same single item, so defeating a boss doesn’t feel genuinely rewarding.    Opinion: as a roguelike, the game has potential, but it needs substantial rebalancing and a deeper rethink of how builds and progression develop.

I've had a blast with this game. When it wants to work (keep that sentiment in mind), messing around with it is incredibly fun, and it can be fascinating to see how broken you can get. So why is this a "not recommended" review?  The answer's quite simple: Gearbox and 2K have their grubby mitts on this game. Between their predatory EULAs and other scummy business practices, not to mention how they've handled the game itself since taking over, Hopoo's legacy and the hard, honest work of those who have worked on Alloyed Collective have effectively been spat on repeatedly. They want control over everything in the IP, and that includes information on those who play it. You're more than welcome to keep going on it if you trust Gearbox and 2K with the stuff on your computer and trust them with every fibre of your being (although why you would want to do that is beyond me). Because, per their EULA, if what they cause via those methods ruins something, you can't hold them liable.  Probably the biggest red flag is that multiplayer itself is completely dysfunctional half of the time. And by "dysfunctional", I mean "it will straight up not load at all, no game for you". Considering that multiplayer is a pretty major pull for a roguelike such as this one, that's just such a big no-no. And as a result of our [i]dear publishers[/i], I don't know - or care - if those problems will be fixed.  Risk Of Rain 2, I love you. I love the way you play and I would have loved to sift through everything Alloyed Collective offers. But I think it's officially time to file for a divorce and head to roguelikes that will actually respect the person playing it.

The items are not game-breaking like in other roguelites, you have nearly zero choice in your build, the enemies are incredibly annoying and tedious to fight, and the bosses are not interesting and have way too much health, making it just a slog to fight. The entire game gets boring after 5 minutes, because fighting the same boring basic enemies becomes repetitive, so I am just forced to ignore them; WHICH, FOR SOME REASON, IS ENTIRELY POSSIBLE. The enemies' attacks are not strong for the most part, but for some reason they have stupidly inflated health and are just time-wasters. The ONLY good things about this game are the character/enemy design and art.

The game itself is really fun. A roguelike with a ton of depth and interesting builds to put together.    Unfortunately it's severely held back by poor connection issues. Unlike many other games, if you get disconnected from a game, there's no way to get back in with your team. I have been playing this game since beta. Few games have given me the sheer elation that this one has, by myself or with friends, on Drizzle or Monsoon, in good times or bad ones. It's truly a marvelous piece of game design that will go down in history as one of the best roguelikes ever created.  And the bugs that were introduced months ago preventing anyone with a controller that doesn't slap into a PS4 or PS5 from playing it reasonably and still haven't been fixed despite multiple tickets being issued on the topic will go down in history as the biggest stain on a game's record since E.T. nearly destroyed the games industry all on its own. No hate to folks who play with mouse and keyboard or even use Playstation controllers, but Gearbox's utter refusal to allow the rest of us to play this game how we like by spending a couple of hours to fix a simple bug is a spit in the face of Hopoo's masterpiece, and of everyone who ever had faith in Gearbox at all.

A long-standing Risk of Rain 2 controller bug from 2020 has resurfaced. After dropping into a run, the game stops recognizing controller inputs for anything except the pause menu. You become stuck in the escape pod because the interact prompt disappears when switching back to the controller, even though the controller works perfectly in all menus. Keyboard input briefly shows an (E) prompt, but controller actions like X/Square do nothing. After hours of troubleshooting, it's clear the issue isn’t related to bindings or Steam controller settings. The bug prevents movement, aiming, abilities, shooting, and interacting, making the game completely unplayable for controller users

The game is fun. I love it deeply. It took over a not insignificant portion of my life, and I still think it's crazy fun. I would easily recommend it to anyone who loves addicting roguelikes in strange alien worlds.  The negative review, however, is because I can't in good conscience support the new owners developing the game. This is a AAA game now, with all the nasty baggage that comes with the term: Pricey DLC, game destroying bugs (retroactively), spyware-enforcing EULAs, the whole kit 'n cabootle. Play if you want, but - even if there are passionate, lovely people working on the game - don't forget that Gearbox as a company is not your friend.  Edit: With the addition of the Alloyed Collective DLC the game is in a better state than it was previously, however I don't feel safe making this a positive review just yet. With everything great it adds, Alloyed Collective is still an expensive DLC that feels more appropriate as an update. Again, play if you so desire, but stay cautious about Gearbox for the time being.
